meta.purpose: Reasoning chains explaining why each KDNA authoring principle is necessary — the 'why' behind the axioms.
meta.load_condition: Load when the user or agent asks why a particular authoring rule exists, or needs rationale to explain a quality decision.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Skipping the interview step produces KDNA that looks correct but encodes zero expert judgment.
logic: When an agent generates KDNA from a topic name, it fills fields using its training data — which contains general knowledge about the topic, not specific expert distinctions.
General knowledge produces axioms like 'Communication is key' and misunderstandings like 'Communication does not matter' — these are plausible-sounding but behaviorally empty.
A user reviewing such output may accept it because it 'sounds right' — creating false confidence that expertise has been encoded when it has not.
Expert distinctions can only come from the expert. The agent's role is extraction (through contrast-seeking questions), not generation (from topic inference).
reasoning_chains.so_what: Every KDNA authoring session must begin with an interview. The agent must not draft any file until at least three specific expert distinctions have been extracted.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: Preventing wrong interpretation is more behaviorally impactful than providing correct information.
logic: A generic model, when facing an ambiguous input, defaults to the most common interpretation — which in specialized domains is often wrong.
Providing correct definitions helps the agent understand the domain but does not actively prevent the most common wrong interpretation.
A misunderstanding entry with 'wrong: X, correct: Y, key distinction: Z' directly intercepts the default wrong interpretation and replaces it with the correct frame.
This is why misunderstandings produce larger behavioral changes than ontology concepts — they target the specific interpretation error the agent is likely to make.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Prioritize misunderstanding extraction during the interview. Every important concept should have a corresponding misunderstanding entry. If the user cannot identify what people commonly get wrong about a concept, the concept may not need to be in the domain.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: A case without an explicit structural pattern cannot be reused — it is a story, not a judgment template.
logic: Stories contain implicit judgment — the reader infers the lesson from the narrative. Different readers extract different lessons.
A KDNA case must make the judgment pattern explicit: what signal was present, what misinterpretation was possible, what correct frame was applied, what action followed.
This explicit structure allows a loading agent to match new situations against the pattern — 'does this new input contain a similar signal to case X?'
Without the structural_pattern field, the agent cannot pattern-match — the case becomes inert reference material rather than an active judgment template.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Every KDNA case must include a structural_pattern field. Audit existing cases: if you cannot extract the signal → misread → correct frame → action chain, the case is a story, not a case.

reasoning_chains.one_sentence: A domain focused on one specific judgment gap produces more behavior change than a domain covering an entire profession.
logic: Broad domains ('sales', 'leadership', 'writing') force the author to write axioms that apply to everything in that field — which means they apply to nothing specific.

Narrow domains ('price objection diagnosis', 'first-time manager feedback', 'argument detection in drafts') force the author to write axioms that apply to specific situations — which means they actually change behavior in those situations.
A broad domain with 20 generic axioms produces less behavioral change than a narrow domain with 4 specific axioms.
Narrow domains can be composed into clusters (via KDNA_Cluster.json) to cover broader areas without losing specificity.
reasoning_chains.so_what: Push the user to narrow the domain scope until each axiom addresses a specific judgment situation. If the domain name is a single broad word, it is too broad. Add a qualifier: 'X within Y context' or 'X vs Y distinction.'
